exemplar.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained focus on the prescribed title with clear links to two areas of knowledge (Art and Natural Sciences)
Well-chosen and authentic examples (Munchâs journal, Glass Beach, bees, embryology) that illustrate the tension between wonder and explanation
Recognition of contrasting perspectives (aesthetic fascination vs. ethical concern; enchantment vs. ecological awareness)
Logical progression of argument with coherent structural bridges between examples
Effective use of a primary source (Munchâs journal) to ground analysis
weaknesses: Uneven depth of analysis and limited interrogation of knowledge-construction processes
Insufficient use of explicit TOK terminology (ways of knowing, knowledge framework)
Counter-claims and broader implications are noted but only briefly evaluated
Inconsistent and sometimes unreliable citations (broken URLs, popular blogs rather than scholarly sources)
Conclusion is largely descriptive and lacks critical reflection on underlying assumptions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment placed the essay firmly in band 5â6, noting its clear focus, relevant real-world examples, and some evaluation of different points of view, but also its uneven depth of analysis and underdeveloped TOK terminology. To match the official total of 6, this single criterion is awarded 6 marks. This reflects the essayâs overall strength in coherence and example selection, tempered by its shortcomings in theoretical grounding, citation rigour, and critical depth.

annotations.comment: The Munch example is well chosen and illustrates how contextual information can deepen our appreciation, effectively linking artistic choices to enriched wonder.
The continuation of Munchâs journal quote effectively grounds the analysis in a primary source, enriching the exampleâs authenticity.
The citation of Munchâs journal entry provides authenticity, but evaluating the sourceâs reliability and perspective would enhance the TOK analysis.
The URL for the UN environment source is broken and lacks bibliographic detail; ensuring accurate and reliable references is essential.
The conclusion synthesizes examples descriptively but stops short of critically evaluating underlying assumptions or considering broader implications for knowledge production.
The student could reference key TOK terminology such as âways of knowingâ and âknowledge frameworkâ here to signal theoretical grounding from the outset.

The opening relies on dictionary definitions and lacks critical engagement with how âwonderâ and âknowledge acquisitionâ function as concepts within TOK; the student should integrate analysis of specific ways of knowing.

The Hirst example is vividly described but analysis remains at the descriptive level; the student should examine how the presentation medium and curatorâs choices influence shared knowledge and wonder.
The Glass Beach example effectively links environmental knowledge to diminished wonder, providing a concrete natural sciences case.
The reflection on individual ecological sensitivity is insightful but could be deepened by discussing how personal values act as filters within the knowledge framework.
The bees example compellingly shows transformation of fear into wonder through knowledge; linking this to emotion and sense perception as ways of knowing would deepen the TOK connection.
The source on propolis is a popular blog rather than a peer-reviewed study; using authoritative scientific literature would improve credibility.
The student clearly restates the prescribed title and outlines the two chosen areas of knowledge, establishing a focused framework for the essay.
The Beloussovitch example engages personally but remains anecdotal and lacks critical perspective; more rigorous evidence and connection to the knowledge framework are needed.
The claim about sardine populations lacks supporting data or scientific references; incorporating empirical studies would substantiate the environmental argument.
Heavy reliance on dictionary sources in footnotes is descriptive; integrating definitions into the main analytical narrative would strengthen the essayâs critical depth.
The ethical counterclaim concerning animal rights is noted but lacks exploration of its implications for knowledge production and the moral responsibilities of the knower in TOK.
The example of Beloussovitch exemplifies how personal context can transform perception of art, demonstrating variation in wonder across audiences.
The studentâs recognition of varied responses among ecologically conscious individuals offers a coherent bridge between examples, enhancing structural flow.
The embryology discussion presents a vivid illustration of wonder in science and could serve to demonstrate specific ways of knowing, such as sense perception and reasoning.
The final summary reiterates previous points without offering new insight or reflection on the implications of wonder for different knower perspectives; deeper evaluation is needed.
While clearly labeled, the bibliography lacks in-text citation consistency and standardized formatting; applying a recognized style guide would enhance professionalism.
The bibliography demonstrates source variety but needs uniform citation formatting and evaluation of each sourceâs authority to meet TOK research standards.
The transition to the natural sciences is abrupt; clearer signposting and explicit linkage between art and science discussions would improve cohesion.
The embryology example is engaging but relies on personal anecdote; citing scholarly sources and discussing scientific methodology would strengthen its academic rigor.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
